F.ISI.ARPA is laid out in full at offset 20; FOO.F.ISI.ARPA at offset 40 reuses it via a pointer to offset 20; ARPA at offset 64 points to offset 26 (the trailing 'ARPA' label inside F.ISI.ARPA); and the root at offset 92 is the single zero-octet that terminates any DNS name.
usage: Used by every DNS implementer as the reference example for getting label compression right. The compression scheme — high two bits of a label-length octet set to 11 to indicate a 14-bit pointer offset — is the most error-prone part of the DNS wire format, and this is the figure CVE write-ups and parser test suites cite when explaining the bugs they found. The example also doubles as a teaching aid for the more general concept of in-message back-references in compact binary encodings.
known_issues: The ASCII-art bit-width conventions in the original RFC are tricky to read: each row is a 16-bit word and the vertical bars at columns 0 and 8 separate the two octets. The figure uses base-10 offsets in the left margin, which beginners sometimes confuse for byte counts within the row. RFC 3597 (2003) clarifies that compression must not be used for unknown record types, a constraint not visible in this 1987 example.
alternatives: RFC 6891 (EDNS) extends the DNS message format with the OPT pseudo-record but does not change compression rules. The DNS-over-HTTPS (RFC 8484) and DNS-over-TLS (RFC 7858) transports wrap classic RFC 1035 messages without altering the compression scheme, so this example remains canonical for the wire format itself.

name: UDHR Article 1 (Latin)
text: Omnes homines dignitate et iure liberi et pares nascuntur, rationis et conscientiae participes sunt, quibus inter se concordiae studio est agendum.
creator: United Nations General Assembly; Latin translation curated by the OHCHR translation project (Latin is not a UN official language)
source: United Nations General Assembly. (1948). Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Resolution 217 A (III). Latin text as collected by the OHCHR UDHR translation project and reproduced byte-for-byte in the Unicode Consortium 'UDHR in Unicode' project (file udhr_lat.txt / udhr_lat.xml, © Office of the High Commissioner for Human Rights).
description: The Latin translation of UDHR Article 1, a 20th-century neo-Latin rendering held in the OHCHR translation collection and preserved in the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' archive. Latin is not a UN working language and the Latin UDHR is a curiosity rather than an instrument of state, but its inclusion in the OHCHR project gives the corpus a verifiable real-Latin natural-language entry to pair directly against Lorem Ipsum, the scrambled-Cicero placeholder. Two clauses of literary Latin with the participial 'participes sunt' construction and the gerundive 'agendum'.
usage: When a researcher needs a short, public-domain real-Latin test text — distinct from Lorem Ipsum's scrambled pseudotext — they reach for UDHR Article 1 in Latin. The most analytically useful contrast in the corpus is the Lorem Ipsum pair: same language, same script, comparable byte length, but real Latin morphology and syntax versus deliberately incoherent placeholder text. Holding article content constant across UDHR siblings isolates the language axis; pairing with Lorem Ipsum isolates the real-vs.-placeholder axis on a single language.
known_issues: This is a 20th-century neo-Latin translation of a 20th-century document, not classical or medieval Latin; the diction reflects modern juridical and ecclesiastical conventions ('iurium' for legal rights, 'concordiae studio' for the gendered 'spirit of brotherhood'). The OHCHR Latin translation was historically published at LangID=ltn on the OHCHR site; that page's address has shifted across OHCHR site reorganizations and the canonical bytes are most reliably accessed through the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' archive, which carries the explicit OHCHR copyright marker. No competing 'official' Latin translation exists.
alternatives: For other compact Latin test texts: Lorem Ipsum (already in the corpus, scrambled Cicero), the Vulgate Pater Noster (Matt. 6:9–13, often used as the historical predecessor of UDHR Article 1 in cross-language linguistic comparison), or short selections from Caesar's Gallic Wars. None of these is a parallel-text family member of UDHR Article 1 — only the OHCHR translation provides that property.

name: UDHR Article 1 (Dutch)
text: Alle mensen worden vrij en gelijk in waardigheid en rechten geboren. Zij zijn begiftigd met verstand en geweten, en behoren zich jegens elkander in een geest van broederschap te gedragen.
creator: United Nations General Assembly; Dutch translation curated by the OHCHR translation project (Dutch is not a UN official language)
source: United Nations General Assembly. (1948). Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Resolution 217 A (III). Dutch text as collected by the OHCHR UDHR translation project, distributed via the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' project (file udhr_nld.txt, © OHCHR), and verified against nl.wikisource.org.
description: The Dutch translation of UDHR Article 1. Dutch is not one of the six UN official languages, but a single canonical translation serves both the Netherlands and Flanders — OHCHR maintains no separate Northern Dutch and Flemish texts. Two sentences in standard mid-20th-century formal Dutch.
usage: When a researcher needs a short, public-domain Dutch test text for cross-lingual NLP and tokenizer evaluation, they reach for UDHR Article 1 in Dutch. Dutch is a useful Germanic neighbour to the German UDHR Article 1: closely related morphology and lexicon ('mensen' / 'Menschen', 'broederschap' / 'Brüderlichkeit', 'waardigheid' / 'Würde'), but no ä/ö/ü/ß diacritics, so cl100k_base treats it more like English byte-wise. The German-Dutch pair isolates the language axis between two Germanic languages with different orthographic surface forms.
known_issues: Although both the Netherlands and the Flemish-speaking community of Belgium use this single text, the lexical choice 'elkander' rather than the modern 'elkaar' gives the passage a slightly archaic register characteristic of mid-20th-century formal Dutch; a contemporary translator would likely write 'elkaar' today. There is no competing 'official' Dutch UDHR translation.
alternatives: The Europarl Dutch-English corpus, the SoNaR Reference Corpus of Contemporary Written Dutch, and the Lassy treebanks are the larger-scale alternatives for Dutch NLP. For Dutch-Flemish dialectal comparison, the CGN (Corpus Gesproken Nederlands) provides spoken-language coverage of both varieties.

name: UDHR Article 1 (Catalan)
text: Tots els éssers humans neixen lliures i iguals en dignitat i en drets. Són dotats de raó i de consciència, i han de comportar-se fraternalment els uns amb els altres.
creator: United Nations General Assembly; Catalan translation curated by the OHCHR translation project (Catalan is not a UN official language)
source: United Nations General Assembly. (1948). Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Resolution 217 A (III). Catalan text as collected by the OHCHR UDHR translation project (filed under ISO 639-3 'cat' as 'Catalan-Valencian-Balear'), distributed via the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' project (file udhr_cat.txt, © OHCHR), and verified against ca.wikisource.org.
description: The Catalan translation of UDHR Article 1, in standard IEC orthography. The OHCHR file is labelled 'Catalan-Valencian-Balear' (the ISO 639-3 macrolanguage grouping), but this is the same standard text used officially across Catalonia, Valencia, and the Balearic Islands. Two sentences in modern literary Catalan.
usage: When a researcher needs a short, public-domain Catalan test text for cross-lingual NLP and tokenizer evaluation, they reach for UDHR Article 1 in Catalan. Catalan is the corpus's first West-Iberian/Occitano-Romance entry, distinct from both the Iberian Spanish and Gallo-Romance French entries — its diacritic inventory (acute and grave accents, the c-cedilla) sits between French and Spanish under cl100k_base. Holding article content constant across Romance siblings isolates the within-family variation cleanly.
known_issues: The OHCHR file groups Catalan, Valencian, and Balearic under a single text; speakers of Valencian in particular sometimes prefer slightly different orthographic forms, and the AVL (Acadèmia Valenciana de la Llengua) maintains a parallel standard with minor divergences. The Article 1 passage as written here uses none of the contested forms and is uncontroversial in either standard.
alternatives: The Catalan AnCora corpus and the OPUS Catalan parallel data are the larger-scale alternatives for Catalan NLP. For Valencian-specific work, the COVAL corpus is the standard reference.

name: UDHR Article 1 (Swahili)
text: Watu wote wamezaliwa huru, hadhi na haki zao ni sawa. Wote wamejaliwa akili na dhamiri, hivyo yapasa watendeane kindugu.
creator: United Nations General Assembly; Swahili (Kiswahili) translation curated by the OHCHR translation project (Swahili is not a UN official language)
source: United Nations General Assembly. (1948). Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Resolution 217 A (III). Swahili text as collected by the OHCHR UDHR translation project, distributed via the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' project (file udhr_swh.txt, © OHCHR). The same wording is the standard East African (Kiswahili) version used by the United Nations Information Centre and the East African Community.
description: The standard Swahili (Kiswahili) translation of UDHR Article 1. Swahili is not one of the six UN official languages but is the lingua franca of much of East Africa and an official language of the African Union. The OHCHR text covers standard East African Kiswahili (ISO 639-3 'swh'); Congolese Swahili ('swc') has its own separate OHCHR translation. Two sentences in standard Kiswahili.
usage: When a researcher needs a short, public-domain Swahili test text for cross-lingual NLP and tokenizer evaluation, they reach for UDHR Article 1 in Swahili. Swahili is the first Bantu / Niger-Congo entry in the corpus and the first sub-Saharan-African language entry of any kind, opening a previously empty cell in the breadth space. Despite being written in plain ASCII Latin script with no diacritics, Swahili tokenizes inefficiently under cl100k_base because Bantu agglutinative morphology produces long compound surface forms (wamezaliwa, watendeane) that the BPE encoder did not see during training. The token-to-character ratio is much higher than for Western European Latin-script languages.
known_issues: Swahili's Bantu noun-class system attaches concord prefixes to nouns, verbs, and adjectives that appear under different orthographic surfaces in the same sentence ('Watu wote' / 'Wote wamejaliwa'); cross-lingual NLP systems trained predominantly on Indo-European languages systematically under-represent these patterns. The Congolese Swahili variant ('swc' in OHCHR) has different lexical choices and is a separate parallel-text family member, not yet in the corpus.
alternatives: For larger-scale Swahili NLP: the OPUS Swahili parallel data, the JW300 corpus (Jehovah's Witnesses parallel text, much larger but with religious-vocabulary bias), and the Helsinki Corpus of Swahili are the standard references.

name: UDHR Article 1 (Indonesian)
text: Semua orang dilahirkan merdeka dan mempunyai martabat dan hak-hak yang sama. Mereka dikaruniai akal dan hati nurani dan hendaknya bergaul satu sama lain dalam semangat persaudaraan.
creator: United Nations General Assembly; Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia) translation curated by the OHCHR translation project (Indonesian is not a UN official language)
source: United Nations General Assembly. (1948). Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Resolution 217 A (III). Indonesian text as collected by the OHCHR UDHR translation project, distributed via the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' project (file udhr_ind.txt, © OHCHR). Distinct from the Malay (ISO 639-3 'msa') translation, which OHCHR maintains as a separate file.
description: The Indonesian (Bahasa Indonesia) translation of UDHR Article 1. Indonesian is not a UN official language but is one of the most-spoken languages in the world and the national language of Indonesia. Distinct from Malaysian Malay despite their shared ancestry — the two have separate OHCHR translation files. Two sentences in standard Bahasa Indonesia.
usage: When a researcher needs a short, public-domain Indonesian test text for cross-lingual NLP and tokenizer evaluation, they reach for UDHR Article 1 in Indonesian. Indonesian is the first Austronesian entry in the corpus, opening a previously empty language-family cell. Indonesian's morphology is largely analytic with productive affixation (di-, -kan, ber-), and its hyphenated reduplication marker ('hak-hak' as the plural of 'hak') is a tokenizer edge case under cl100k_base. Holding article content constant against Malay (when added) would isolate the Malayic dialect axis cleanly.
known_issues: Indonesian and Malaysian Malay are mutually intelligible but lexically and orthographically distinct enough that NLP work must choose between them deliberately; OHCHR's separate ISO 639-3 'msa' file is the Malay sister entry. Indonesian's hyphenated reduplication is sometimes mishandled by tokenizers that treat the hyphen as a word boundary. The text uses no language-specific diacritics.
alternatives: For larger-scale Indonesian NLP: the IndoNLP collection, the OSCAR Indonesian subcorpus, and the Indonesian Universal Dependencies treebank (IndoUD) are the standard references.

name: UDHR Article 1 (Vietnamese)
text: Tất cả mọi người sinh ra đều được tự do và bình đẳng về nhân phẩm và quyền. Mọi con người đều được tạo hoá ban cho lý trí và lương tâm và cần phải đối xử với nhau trong tình bằng hữu.
creator: United Nations General Assembly; Vietnamese translation curated by the OHCHR translation project (Vietnamese is not a UN official language)
source: United Nations General Assembly. (1948). Universal Declaration of Human Rights, Resolution 217 A (III). Vietnamese text as collected by the OHCHR UDHR translation project, distributed via the Unicode 'UDHR in Unicode' project (file udhr_vie.txt, © OHCHR). The same wording is used by the Vietnamese Ministry of Justice.
description: The Vietnamese translation of UDHR Article 1. Vietnamese is not a UN official language but is one of the most-spoken languages in Southeast Asia. Written in chữ Quốc ngữ — the Latin-based alphabet introduced by Portuguese and French missionaries in the 17th century and standardized in the early 20th — with extensive diacritic marks for tone and vowel quality. Two sentences in standard Vietnamese.
usage: When a researcher needs a short, public-domain Vietnamese test text for cross-lingual NLP and tokenizer evaluation, they reach for UDHR Article 1 in Vietnamese. Vietnamese is the first Austroasiatic entry in the corpus and the most diacritic-dense Latin-script language available — every syllable can carry up to two diacritic marks (one for vowel quality, one for tone), often producing precomposed Unicode characters that span multiple combining-character classes. The token count under cl100k_base is dramatically inflated relative to text length: 84 tokens for a passage shorter in characters than the German UDHR Article 1 at 44 tokens, making Vietnamese the strongest tokenizer-stress example currently in the corpus. The text is in NFC normalization (precomposed diacritics).
known_issues: Vietnamese diacritics can be encoded in either NFC (precomposed) or NFD (decomposed combining-mark) form; this entry preserves the OHCHR file's NFC encoding. Tokenizers and embedding models that internally re-normalize to NFD will see different byte sequences. The chữ Quốc ngữ orthography is standardized but pre-1975 Northern and Southern lexical conventions differ slightly; the OHCHR text uses contemporary Vietnamese.
alternatives: For larger-scale Vietnamese NLP: the OPUS Vietnamese parallel data, the VLSP corpora (Vietnamese Language and Speech Processing project), and the VnCoreNLP toolkit's bundled corpora are the standard references.

name: Newton's Axiomata sive Leges Motus
text:
Axiomata sive Leges Motus.

Lex. I.
Corpus omne perseverare in statu suo quiescendi vel movendi uniformiter in directum, nisi quatenus a viribus impressis cogitur statum illum mutare.

Lex. II.
Mutationem motus proportionalem esse vi motrici impressæ, & fieri secundum lineam rectam qua vis illa imprimitur.

Lex. III.
Actioni contrariam semper & æqualem esse reactionem: sive corporum duorum actiones in se mutuo semper esse æquales & in partes contrarias dirigi.
creator: Isaac Newton
source: Newton, Isaac. Philosophiae Naturalis Principia Mathematica, Book I, "Axiomata sive Leges Motus." London, 1687 (first edition). Transcribed from Project Gutenberg ebook #28233.
description: Newton's three Laws of Motion as he stated them in the Axiomata section opening Book I of the Principia (1687): the law of inertia, the law of force and acceleration, and the law of action and reaction. The founding axiomatization of classical mechanics, in Newton's original Latin.
usage: The canonical primary source for citations of Newton's Laws in physics, history of science, and classical-mechanics pedagogy. Also serves as a Latin-script natural-language sample drawn from the early-modern scientific register, distinct from Lorem Ipsum (Renaissance typesetting filler) and the UDHR Latin translation (mid-20th-century formal-legal style).
known_issues: Three Principia editions exist (1687, 1713, 1726); the Latin text of Lex I–III is essentially identical across them. The entry omits Newton's own explanatory paragraph that follows each Lex statement (commentary on the law, not the law itself), as well as the Corollaria and Scholium that close the Axiomata section. Capitalisation of the section heading is normalised from the source's three-line all-caps title block (AXIOMATA / SIVE / LEGES MOTUS) to the conventional inline form, and Project Gutenberg's italic-marker underscores are stripped.
alternatives: English translations: Andrew Motte (1729), Cohen & Whitman (1999). Modern physics textbooks almost always paraphrase the laws in their own language rather than quote the Latin verbatim.

name: Sanger's Bovine Insulin Sequence
text:
A: GIVEQCCASVCSLYQLENYCN
B: FVNQHLCGSHLVEALYLVCGERGFFYTPKA
creator: Frederick Sanger and colleagues
source: Ryle, A. P., Sanger, F., Smith, L. F., & Kitai, R. (1955). "The disulphide bonds of insulin." Biochemical Journal 60(4): 541–556. Sequence as currently catalogued in UniProt P01317 (INS_BOVIN): mature A-chain (residues 85–105) and B-chain (residues 25–54) of the bovine preproinsulin precursor.
description: The mature bovine insulin protein in modern one-letter amino-acid codes: A-chain (21 residues) and B-chain (30 residues), totalling 51 residues across two chains held together by disulfide bonds. The first protein ever fully sequenced — Sanger's 1951–1955 work, for which he received the 1958 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
usage: The founding artifact of protein sequencing and proteomics. Used as a historical reference in biochemistry pedagogy, as a small canonical protein in structural-biology pipelines (paired with PDB structures such as 2INS), and as a tokeniser/embedding probe for amino-acid letter sequences distinct from DNA letter sequences.
known_issues: The linear amino-acid sequence does not capture the three disulfide bonds (A6–A11 intrachain; A7–B7 and A20–B19 interchain) that define insulin's tertiary structure and biological activity. Bovine insulin differs from human insulin at three positions (A8, A10, B30). Sanger's original 1951–1955 papers used three-letter amino-acid codes and full residue names rather than the one-letter codes shown here, which became standard later (IUPAC-IUB 1968).
alternatives: UniProt P01317 (full bovine preproinsulin precursor, 105 aa, including signal peptide and C-peptide); PDB 2INS (bovine insulin crystal structure); UniProt P01308 (human insulin, modern clinical reference).

name: Caffeine SMILES
text: CN1C=NC2=C1C(=O)N(C(=O)N2C)C
creator: David Weininger (SMILES notation); molecular structure of caffeine determined long before
source: PubChem CID 2519 (caffeine), SMILES property, U.S. National Library of Medicine. SMILES notation defined in: Weininger, D. (1988). "SMILES, a chemical language and information system. 1. Introduction to methodology and encoding rules." Journal of Chemical Information and Computer Sciences 28(1): 31–36.
description: The canonical SMILES (Simplified Molecular-Input Line-Entry System) string for caffeine (1,3,7-trimethylxanthine, C8H10N4O2), as catalogued by PubChem. SMILES is the dominant line notation for representing molecular structures in cheminformatics and computational chemistry.
usage: The standard machine-readable representation of caffeine for cheminformatics tools (RDKit, OpenBabel, ChEMBL queries, PubChem lookups, ML featurisation). Caffeine is a conventional 'hello-world' molecule in SMILES tutorials and tokenizer/embedding tests because it is small, planar, achiral, biologically familiar, and exercises ring numbering, branching, and aromatic-vs-Kekulé conventions in a single short string.
known_issues: SMILES is non-unique: multiple valid strings encode the same molecule, and different toolkits canonicalise differently (PubChem/Daylight, RDKit, OpenSMILES, OpenEye each produce distinct 'canonical' forms). PubChem renamed this property from 'CanonicalSMILES' to 'SMILES' (with a sister 'ConnectivitySMILES' field) in a recent schema update. The string does not encode stereochemistry (caffeine has none) or distinguish tautomers.
alternatives: InChI: InChI=1S/C8H10N4O2/c1-10-4-9-6-5(10)7(13)12(3)8(14)11(6)2/h4H,1-3H3. InChIKey: RYYVLZVUVIJVGH-UHFFFAOYSA-N. Other line notations: SMARTS, SLN, SYBYL, Wiswesser line-notation (WLN, historical).

name: goto fail (CVE-2014-1266)
creator: Apple Inc.
source: Apple Inc. Security framework, ssl/sslKeyExchange.c, function SSLVerifySignedServerKeyExchange, lines 625-633 of the Security-55471 release. Disclosed February 2014 as CVE-2014-1266.
description: The canonical 'famous because of a flaw' code excerpt: nine lines from Apple's TLS implementation containing a duplicated, unconditional `goto fail;` statement that bypassed the entire subsequent signature-verification step in iOS and OS X TLS handshakes. The bug was disclosed as CVE-2014-1266 and is universally referred to in the secure-coding literature by the name of the buggy line itself: 'goto fail'.
usage: When a secure-coding instructor needs the textbook example of how a single duplicated line can break TLS authentication, they reach for this exact excerpt from sslKeyExchange.c. It is the standard case study for the dangers of brace-less if-statements, the value of mandatory braces in style guides (Apple's response was enforcement of MISRA-C-style brace rules), and the failure modes of cleanup-via-goto in C. Reproduced verbatim in essentially every post-2014 textbook on secure C programming and in Adam Langley's widely-cited contemporaneous writeup.
known_issues: The excerpt is the bug site, not the entire vulnerable function. The function SSLVerifySignedServerKeyExchange continues for another 20 lines after the shown excerpt, including the call to sslRawVerify that is the actual signature check the duplicated goto bypasses. Reproductions vary in how much surrounding context they include; the nine-line form here is the minimum that lets the bug be self-evident.
alternatives: The closest contemporary analog is Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160), disclosed two months later in OpenSSL — also a missing-check bug in TLS handshake code, also from 2014, also producing authentication/disclosure failure from a small omission. The pair is the canonical 'two 2014 TLS bugs' cluster in security curricula.

name: Heartbleed (CVE-2014-0160)
creator: OpenSSL Project (Robin Seggelmann, original author of the patch)
source: OpenSSL ssl/t1_lib.c, function tls1_process_heartbeat, lines 2553-2587 of the OpenSSL_1_0_1f release tag. The vulnerable code was introduced in commit 4817504 (2011) and shipped in OpenSSL 1.0.1 through 1.0.1f. Disclosed April 2014 as CVE-2014-0160.
description: The canonical Heartbleed excerpt: 35 lines of OpenSSL's tls1_process_heartbeat function showing the bug from the function signature through the unchecked memcpy. The attacker-controlled `payload` length is read from the heartbeat record header at `n2s(p, payload);` and then used at `memcpy(bp, pl, payload);` to copy that many bytes into the response buffer — with no check that `pl` actually has `payload` bytes available. The result was the disclosure of up to 64KB of process memory per request, including private keys.
usage: When a security instructor needs the textbook example of a missing bounds check in security-critical code, they reach for this exact excerpt from tls1_process_heartbeat. It is the standard case study for memory-safety failure in C, for the danger of trusting attacker-supplied length fields, and for the broader 'why memory-safe languages' argument. Reproduced verbatim in every post-2014 secure-coding textbook and in the Codenomicon and Google Project Zero writeups that announced the disclosure.
known_issues: Different writeups quote different excerpt sizes. The minimum that contains the bug is two non-adjacent lines (`n2s(p, payload);` and `memcpy(bp, pl, payload);`); the form here includes the function signature, the request branch entry, and the malloc that also trusts the unchecked length, which is the smallest excerpt that lets the missing-check structure be self-evident without elision. The function continues for another ~30 lines after the shown excerpt to handle the response branch and free buffers.
alternatives: Pairs directly with goto fail (CVE-2014-1266), disclosed two months earlier in Apple's TLS stack — also a missing-check bug in handshake code from 2014. The two bugs together are the canonical 'two 2014 TLS disasters' contrast in security curricula: a missing branch versus a missing length check.

name: Duff's Device
creator: Tom Duff
source: Duff, T. (1983, November 10). Internal memorandum at Lucasfilm, subsequently posted to USENET newsgroup net.lang.c in May 1984. Archived at lysator.liu.se/c/duffs-device.html with the original message headers.
description: Tom Duff's loop-unrolling switch-case interleaving from 1983, the canonical example of legal C that looks illegal. The device interleaves a `switch` statement with a `do-while` loop in a way that exploits C's switch fall-through and the absence of structural restrictions on where `case` labels can appear, producing an unrolled byte-copy loop from arbitrary count. Duff wrote it at Lucasfilm to speed up real-time graphics output and described it on USENET with the now-famous closing line: 'I feel a combination of pride and revulsion at this discovery.'
usage: When a C compiler writer or language lawyer needs the canonical example of a construct that the standard permits but human readers do not believe is legal, they reach for Duff's Device. It is in the GCC test suite (where every conforming C compiler must accept it), reproduced in K&R-adjacent C textbooks as the definitive 'why switch fall-through is in the language' example, and cited in every discussion of structured programming's limits. The Jargon File entry is the standard secondary reference.
known_issues: The original 1983 form uses pre-ANSI K&R declaration syntax (implicit int, no parameter types in the function header) and compiles only with warnings under modern C standards. Modern ANSI/ISO C versions of the device exist but are not the canonical text. There is also no explicit license statement on Duff's writings or on the lysator archive; the code has been reproduced for forty years without any restriction asserted by Duff, including in the GCC test suite, the Jargon File, Wikipedia, and most C compiler textbooks.
alternatives: Modern compilers will perform equivalent loop unrolling automatically when given a straightforward `for` loop, so the device has no remaining performance use. As a *teaching* example of switch fall-through it has no real substitute — later attempts to make the same point (the 'computed goto' extensions in GCC, Protothreads in Adam Dunkels' work) all trace their lineage to it.

name: Colorless Green Ideas Sleep Furiously
text: Colorless green ideas sleep furiously.
creator: Noam Chomsky
source: Chomsky, N. (1957). Syntactic Structures. The Hague: Mouton & Co. Sentence (1) of Chapter 2, page 15.
description: The five-word sentence Chomsky introduced on page 15 of Syntactic Structures (1957) as evidence that grammaticality and meaningfulness are independent: the sentence is syntactically well-formed by every English rule but semantically anomalous, with no coherent interpretation. Chomsky paired it with the ungrammatical permutation 'Furiously sleep ideas green colorless' to argue that no statistical model of word co-occurrence could explain why a native speaker finds the first acceptable and the second not.
usage: When a linguist or philosopher of language needs the canonical example of a syntactically well-formed semantically anomalous English sentence, they reach for this exact five-word string. It is in every introductory syntax textbook from Akmajian through Carnie, the standard worked example for the syntax/semantics distinction, and the standard probe for selectional-restriction failures in computational semantics. It is also one of the most-cited sentences in 20th-century linguistics, second perhaps only to 'The cat sat on the mat'.
known_issues: Subsequent writers have constructed contexts in which the sentence does have an interpretation — most famously the 1985 Stanford 'Colorless Green Ideas' contest collected by John Hollander, with prize entries giving the line a metaphorical poetic reading. These do not undermine Chomsky's point: the question is whether the sentence is interpretable *out of context* by ordinary compositional semantics, and the consensus answer remains no. The sentence is also sometimes wrongly attributed to earlier writers; Chomsky 1957 is the standard citation.
alternatives: Lewis Carroll's Jabberwocky stanza 1 (1871) is the other canonical English example in this space, but it makes a different point: Jabberwocky uses real morphology with nonce lexemes, while 'Colorless green ideas' uses real lexemes with anomalous selectional combinations. The two together cover the morphology/semantics axes of the well-formedness question. 'The horse raced past the barn fell' (Bever 1970) is the complementary garden-path example — well-formed *and* meaningful, but parsed wrong by humans on first pass.
related: The Horse Raced Past the Barn Fell

name: The Horse Raced Past the Barn Fell
text: The horse raced past the barn fell.
creator: Thomas G. Bever
source: Bever, T. G. (1970). The cognitive basis for linguistic structures. In J. R. Hayes (Ed.), Cognition and the Development of Language (pp. 279-362). New York: Wiley.
description: The eight-word garden-path sentence Bever introduced in 1970 to demonstrate that the human sentence parser commits early to a single structural analysis and reanalyzes only when forced to. On first pass, readers parse 'The horse raced past the barn' as a complete clause and then stumble at 'fell'; the resolution requires reparsing 'raced past the barn' as a reduced relative clause modifying 'horse' (i.e., 'the horse [that was] raced past the barn fell'). It is the canonical garden-path sentence in psycholinguistics.
usage: When a psycholinguist or NLP researcher needs the textbook example of a syntactically valid sentence that humans parse wrong on first pass, they reach for this exact eight-word string. It is the canonical stimulus for self-paced reading and eyetracking studies of reanalysis cost, the canonical input for testing whether a parser handles reduced relative clauses, and the standard demonstration for the minimal-attachment and late-closure parsing principles in introductory psycholinguistics courses.
known_issues: The sentence is occasionally given with a comma after 'barn' or with 'that was' explicit, both of which destroy the garden-path effect by signaling the reduced-relative reading in advance. The canonical form has no comma and no overt relativizer. Some sources misattribute the sentence to Bransford or to later researchers; Bever 1970 is the standard citation. The effect also weakens for readers who have seen the sentence before — the example is so widely taught that many linguistics students are immune to the garden path on encountering it in textbooks.
alternatives: 'The old man the boat' is a five-word minimal-pair garden-path with the same lexical-category reanalysis structure (man as verb, not noun). 'The complex houses married and single soldiers and their families' is the longer canonical alternative. For the broader class of well-formedness probes: Chomsky's 'Colorless green ideas sleep furiously' (1957) is the syntactic-but-anomalous complement to this syntactic-and-misparsed example.

creator: Edgar Anderson (data collection, 1935); Ronald A. Fisher (statistical analysis, 1936); Michael Marshall (1988 contribution to UCI Machine Learning Repository)
source: Anderson, E. (1935). "The Irises of the Gaspe Peninsula." Bulletin of the American Iris Society 59: 2-5. Fisher, R. A. (1936). "The use of multiple measurements in taxonomic problems." Annals of Eugenics 7(2): 179-188. The bag of bytes shipped here is the UCI Machine Learning Repository file `iris.data`, donated to UCI by Michael Marshall in July 1988 and distributed unchanged ever since (sourced via the Wayback Machine snapshot of https://archive.ics.uci.edu/ml/machine-learning-databases/iris/iris.data, 2023-12-17, 4551 bytes, sha256 6f608b71a7317216319b4d27b4d9bc84e6abd734eda7872b71a458569e2656c0).
description: The UCI Machine Learning Repository's `iris.data` file: 150 specimens (50 each of *Iris setosa*, *I. versicolor*, *I. virginica*) measured by Edgar Anderson in 1935 along four morphological dimensions (sepal length, sepal width, petal length, petal width, in centimetres), distributed verbatim as comma-separated values without a header. The single most-loaded dataset in applied machine learning pedagogy: scikit-learn's `sklearn.datasets.load_iris()` returns these exact 150 rows, every introductory classifier tutorial demonstrates against this file, and tens of thousands of papers cite it as their first sanity-check benchmark. Canonicity is downstream of a specific historical bag of bytes, not of Fisher's underlying paper - the UCI version contains two well-known data-entry discrepancies (instances 35 and 38) that diverge from Fisher's published table; Bezdek et al. (1999) published a corrected version that essentially nobody uses. The errors are the fingerprint that disambiguates 'the Iris dataset' as concept from `iris.data` as artifact, and they are why the entry qualifies under the deficit-defined-canonicity lens (texts that are canonical *because of* a flaw or quirk that any substitute would have differently).
usage: When a machine-learning practitioner needs a small, low-dimensional, multivariate tabular dataset to demonstrate a classifier, fit a clustering algorithm, draw a pairs plot, or sanity-check a feature-selection routine, they reach for this exact 4551-byte CSV file with its 150 rows, no header, `Iris-setosa` / `Iris-versicolor` / `Iris-virginica` labels, and the two row-35 / row-38 discrepancies from Fisher's original paper. It is the canonical 'first dataset' of applied ML, embedded verbatim in scikit-learn (`sklearn/datasets/data/iris.csv`, with a header line added), R's built-in `datasets::iris`, MATLAB's Statistics Toolbox, Weka, and essentially every other ML library. For embedding-model probing it is the corpus's first `category=dataset` exemplar, which is the 'rectangular tabular data with mixed continuous and categorical columns' shape that has otherwise been entirely absent.
known_issues: Two well-documented data-entry errors versus Fisher's 1936 published table: instance 35 (a setosa) is `4.9,3.1,1.5,0.1` in iris.data but `4.9,3.1,1.5,0.2` in Fisher; instance 38 (also a setosa) is `4.9,3.1,1.5,0.1` in iris.data but `4.9,3.6,1.4,0.1` in Fisher. Bezdek, J. C., Keller, J. M., Krisnapuram, R., Kuncheva, L. I., & Pal, N. R. (1999, 'Will the real iris data please stand up?', IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems 7(3): 368-369) published a corrected version, but virtually all modern usage continues to be of the uncorrected UCI bytes - which is precisely why those bytes are the canonical artifact and the corrected version is not. Beyond the errors: *Iris setosa* is linearly separable from the other two species along petal dimensions, which makes the dataset trivially easy for the simplest classifiers and gives a misleadingly optimistic impression of how 'three-class classification' generalises (this is the standard pedagogical critique). The species names in the file are written `Iris-setosa` etc., with a hyphen; the botanical convention is *Iris setosa* (no hyphen, italicised). The 50/50/50 balance is from the experimental design and does not reflect any natural prevalence. Anderson collected the *I. setosa* specimens on the Gaspé Peninsula, Quebec, in 1935; the other two species were collected from the same colony, which limits geographic and ecological diversity. Licensing: the data themselves (measurements + labels) are facts and not copyrightable in most jurisdictions; the UCI Machine Learning Repository has distributed the file freely for research and education since 1988 without explicit licence text on the file, and downstream redistribution by scikit-learn, R, and others has been unchallenged for decades. A minor faithfulness caveat to the bag-of-bytes claim: the canonical UCI file ends with two trailing newline bytes (4551 bytes total); pydantic's `str_strip_whitespace=True` setting on the `Entry` model strips these on validation, so the `text` field stores 4549 bytes ending at the final non-whitespace character. The two missing bytes are insignificant in any CSV consumer (the file parses identically with or without them), but readers wanting absolute byte-equality with the historical UCI distribution should re-add `\n\n` to the end.
alternatives: Bezdek et al. (1999) corrected `bezdekIris.data`, also distributed by UCI alongside the canonical `iris.data` for completeness; Fisher's 1936 published table (the historical primary source); R's built-in `datasets::iris` (a processed `data.frame` with column names `Sepal.Length`, `Sepal.Width`, `Petal.Length`, `Petal.Width`, `Species`, factor-encoded species, and one further small discrepancy from `iris.data`); scikit-learn's bundled `sklearn/datasets/data/iris.csv` (header line added: '150,4,setosa,versicolor,virginica'); MATLAB's `fisheriris` (separate `meas` and `species` arrays). For other classic small tabular ML datasets: Anscombe's quartet (4 pairs of (x, y), each with the same summary statistics but different shapes — the canonical dataset for showing why you must visualise data); the Old Faithful eruptions dataset; the Palmer Penguins dataset (a deliberate modern replacement for Iris that avoids the hyphenation, the colonial-era nomenclature, and the linear-separability issue).
